text: Following the induction of pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon observes the cecum firmly adherent to the anterior lateral abdominal wall. Using harmonic shears, the omental adhesions are divided, leaving the cecum end block. The dissection begins with the identification of the ileocolic pedicle, creating a window near the mesenteric root between the ileocolic vessels and the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon carefully dissects the ileocolic vein down to its junction with the superior mesenteric vein. Using a right angle instrument and Maryland forceps, the vein is isolated and clipped at the confluence with the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic artery passing anterior to the vein and applies double clips at its origin from the superior mesenteric artery. This high ligation of the ileocolic vessels is performed to ensure complete removal of regional lymphatic vessels and lymph nodes, optimizing oncological outcomes.
The surgeon dissects the ascending colonic mesentery in a cranial direction using a medial to lateral approach. The right colic artery is identified and divided during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the distal small bowel mesentery using harmonic shears. Subsequently, the small bowel is transected with an Echelon-60 stapler, positioned 25 centimeters from the ileocecal valve.

The surgeon carefully dissects to separate the cecal mass from the anterior lateral abdominal wall superiorly and the right fallopian tube and external iliac vessels inferiorly. A small peri-appendicular abscess is found and aspirated. The surgeon resects a segment of parietal peritoneum and lateral abdominal wall, removing them en bloc with the specimen. The dissection is completed in the retroperitoneal plane, which remains uninfiltrated by the tumor.

The surgeon identifies and preserves the right ureter, completing the dissection by fully mobilizing the hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon. Care is taken to avoid injury to the adjacent duodenum and head of the pancreas.

The surgeon divides the colon between the proximal and mid-transverse sections. The specimen is extracted through a nine-centimeter phenenstyl incision with partial division of the rectus muscles. The incision is closed, and pneumoperitoneum is re-established. An isoperistaltic ileocolic site-to-site anastomosis is performed, ensuring the anastomosis is well vascularized and tension-free.

The surgeon places two stay sutures and creates enterotomies in both the ileum and colon using hook diathermy and Maryland forceps. The anastomosis is completed with an Echelon-60 stapler. The enterotomy is then closed in two layers: the first layer with interrupted Vicryl stitches and the second serosal layer with running self-locking sutures.
